body: This issue genuinely surprised me. The editorial on memory and place hit closer to home than I expected — I grew up in a city that no longer exists in the way I remember it. The way the piece holds grief and nostalgia at arm's length without dismissing either one is rare in print.
Beautifully said. I felt the same — there's a restraint to the writing that makes it land harder, not softer.

I'd push back gently on that read. I thought the restraint sometimes tipped into distance — a few passages felt like the writer was observing grief rather than inside it. Still a strong piece, just not flawless.

Quick question for anyone who has read the full archive: is the visual language consistent across all issues, or does it shift dramatically? The layout in this one felt very considered — almost architectural.
The grid shifts significantly in later issues — issue 3 onwards gets much looser. This first one is the most structured. Worth reading them in order if you can.
مقال رائع. أتمنى أن يُترجم إلى العربية حتى يصل إلى جمهور أوسع — المحتوى يستحق ذلك بجدارة.
أتفق تماماً. الترجمة ستفتح الباب لقراء كثيرين لم يتعرضوا لهذا النوع من الكتابة من قبل.
Honest take: the photography outshines the writing this issue. Not a criticism of the text — the images are just extraordinary. The spread on pages 18–21 stopped me for a long time.
Those pages stopped me too. Do you know who the photographer is? I didn't see a byline.
It's credited in the colophon at the back — Reem Al-Zahrani. Well worth looking up her other work.

First time reader here. Found this through a link shared in a reading group. Subscribed immediately after finishing. The combination of literary and visual content is exactly what I've been looking for.
Welcome! The back issues are all worth reading in order — each one builds on the last in ways that aren't always obvious until later.

One thing I'd love to see in future issues: longer conversations with the contributors. The brief bio notes are fine but I always want to know more about the process behind a piece — especially the translated work.

Received my print copy today. The paper stock and binding feel premium — you can tell care went into the physical object, not just the content. Stacking this alongside Granta and The Paris Review without hesitation.

I've been following Al-Riwayat since the very first issue. What strikes me about this one is how the theme of displacement is threaded across every piece — even the ones that don't explicitly name it. That kind of editorial cohesion is hard to achieve.
Agreed on the thematic cohesion. The poem on page 34 does it most quietly — it's ostensibly about a garden but reads entirely differently once you've absorbed the rest of the issue.
